content.text: Standard attention is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound. The bottleneck is not the number of floating-point operations but the volume of data moving between GPU HBM (high-bandwidth memory) and the compute units. FlashAttention is described as IO-aware because its design is driven entirely by minimising HBM transfers rather than minimising FLOPs. This section explains why the IO bottleneck exists and how the tiling strategy eliminates it.
Modern GPUs have two main memory levels relevant to attention:
40–80 GB, bandwidth ~2 TB/s on A100. All model weights, activations, and the KV-cache live here. Reads/writes to HBM are the primary bottleneck for memory-bound kernels.
~20 MB total across all SMs, bandwidth ~19 TB/s — about 9× faster than HBM. Data in SRAM can be reused at near-compute speed with no additional HBM traffic.

The gap between HBM bandwidth (2 TB/s) and peak compute (312 TFLOP/s on A100 BF16) gives an arithmetic intensity threshold of about 312/2 = 156 FLOP/byte. A kernel must perform at least 156 FLOPs per byte transferred from HBM to be compute-bound rather than memory-bound.

attrs.alt: GPU memory hierarchy: standard attention vs FlashAttention IO pattern

content.text: . A naïve implementation in PyTorch or cuDNN materialises the full N×N score matrix S and the probability matrix P in HBM. The IO cost at each step:
Read S, write P = softmax(S) to HBM:
Read P, V from HBM, write O to HBM:
For practical values (N = 4096, d = 64, FP16 = 2 bytes):
With 32 attention heads, a single forward pass transfers about 2 GB through HBM — at 2 TB/s that takes ~1 ms just for data movement, independent of computation. The arithmetic intensity is roughly
FLOPs/byte, which for d = 64 is only 64 — well below the 156 threshold, confirming that standard attention is heavily memory-bound.

The key observation is that softmax is the only operation that prevents computing attention entirely in SRAM: it requires the full row of S to compute the denominator. FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) overcomes this with
— an algorithm that computes softmax incrementally as K blocks are streamed through, maintaining a running maximum and normalisation constant.
The tiling strategy divides the computation as follows:
For each outer block (Q tile), iterate over all K, V tiles. All arithmetic happens in SRAM; only the final output tile O is written to HBM.
are chosen so that one Q tile and one K, V tile together fit in SRAM:
where M is the SRAM size in elements. A typical choice on A100 is B_r = B_c = 64 or 128 depending on d.
requires two passes: one to find the maximum for numerical stability, one to compute the exponents and sum. Online softmax merges these into a single pass by maintaining a running state:
After processing all T_c K, V blocks, the output tile is normalised: O_i ← O_i / l_{T_c}. The running maximum m_j and sum l_j are kept in registers (not HBM), adding negligible cost.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def flash_attention_reference(Q: torch.Tensor, K: torch.Tensor, V: torch.Tensor,
                               Br: int = 64, Bc: int = 64) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Pure-Python reference implementation of FlashAttention tiling.
    Numerically equivalent to standard attention, but never materialises the full N×N matrix.
    Q, K, V: [N, d] (single head, no batch dim for clarity)
    """
    N, d = Q.shape
    scale = d ** -0.5
    O = torch.zeros(N, d, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)
    l = torch.zeros(N, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)     # normalisation denominators
    m = torch.full((N,), float("-inf"), dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)  # running max

    Tr = (N + Br - 1) // Br
    Tc = (N + Bc - 1) // Bc

    for i in range(Tr):
        Qi = Q[i*Br : (i+1)*Br]               # [Br, d] — this tile stays "in SRAM"
        Oi = O[i*Br : (i+1)*Br]
        li = l[i*Br : (i+1)*Br]
        mi = m[i*Br : (i+1)*Br]

        for j in range(Tc):
            Kj = K[j*Bc : (j+1)*Bc]           # [Bc, d] — stream K, V tiles
            Vj = V[j*Bc : (j+1)*Bc]           # [Bc, d]

            Sij = (Qi @ Kj.T) * scale          # [Br, Bc] attention scores — in registers
            mij = Sij.max(dim=-1).values        # [Br]

            m_new = torch.maximum(mi, mij)
            Pij = torch.exp(Sij - m_new.unsqueeze(-1))     # [Br, Bc] unnormalised
            lij = Pij.sum(dim=-1)

            # Rescale previous accumulation and add current block
            alpha = torch.exp(mi - m_new)      # correction factor [Br]
            Oi = alpha.unsqueeze(-1) * Oi + Pij @ Vj
            li = alpha * li + lij
            mi = m_new

        O[i*Br : (i+1)*Br] = Oi / li.unsqueeze(-1)   # normalise

    return O

The Python reference above runs in O(N²d) FLOPs — identical to standard attention — but never writes more than O(N d) elements to HBM (the input and output tiles). The real FlashAttention is implemented as a CUDA kernel using shared memory; the Python version here demonstrates the correctness of the algorithm.
, the total HBM IO of FlashAttention is:
where M is the SRAM size in elements. The improvement ratio over standard attention:

On A100, M ≈ 10M elements and d = 64, so the theoretical IO reduction is ~150×. In practice, other overheads limit the gain, but the asymptotic scaling still makes FlashAttention dramatically more memory-efficient at long sequence lengths:
Arithmetic intensity (AI) = FLOPs / bytes transferred from HBM. Comparing the two implementations:
FLOPs/byte. For d = 64 this is 64 FLOP/byte — well below A100's compute:BW ratio of ~156 FLOP/byte. The kernel is memory-bound.
FLOPs/byte. M ≈ 10M elements gives AI ≈ 40M FLOP/byte — far above the compute-bound threshold. The kernel is compute-bound.
This is the fundamental improvement: FlashAttention shifts attention from memory-bound to compute-bound, extracting the full compute potential of the GPU.

def attention_arithmetic_intensity(N: int, d: int, M_elements: int = 10_000_000) -> dict:
    """
    Compute arithmetic intensity (FLOPs per HBM byte) for standard vs FlashAttention.
    M_elements: SRAM size in float16 elements (A100 ≈ 10M)
    """
    flops = 4 * N * N * d                         # same for both (2*N²d matmul + softmax)

    # Standard: IO dominated by N×N matrix (simplified, in elements)
    io_standard = 4 * N * N + 2 * N * d           # read/write S, P + Q, K, V, O
    ai_standard = flops / (io_standard * 2)        # 2 bytes per float16 element

    # FlashAttention: IO is N²d/M elements (approximate, from the paper bound)
    io_flash = max(N * N * d / M_elements, 2 * 3 * N * d)  # at least read Q,K,V
    ai_flash = flops / (io_flash * 2)

    return {
        "N": N,
        "d": d,
        "flops": flops,
        "standard_io_MB": io_standard * 2 / 1e6,
        "flash_io_MB": io_flash * 2 / 1e6,
        "standard_ai": ai_standard,
        "flash_ai": ai_flash,
        "is_flash_compute_bound": ai_flash > 156,   # A100 threshold in FLOP/byte
    }

for N in [512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192]:
    r = attention_arithmetic_intensity(N, d=64)
    print(f"N={r['N']:5d}  std_io={r['standard_io_MB']:7.1f}MB  "
          f"flash_io={r['flash_io_MB']:5.2f}MB  "
          f"std_ai={r['standard_ai']:5.1f}  flash_ai={r['flash_ai']:8.0f}  "
          f"compute-bound={r['is_flash_compute_bound']}")

Standard attention is quadratic in both compute and IO; FlashAttention is quadratic in compute but sub-quadratic in IO. At N = 8192, FlashAttention uses ~600× less HBM bandwidth, enabling contexts that would otherwise OOM.

The full attention matrix is never stored in HBM. This eliminates the O(N²) memory footprint and enables gradient checkpointing for backward pass (recomputing attention in chunks rather than storing it).
FlashAttention is numerically equivalent to standard attention (up to floating-point rounding), not an approximation. There is no quality trade-off.
All steps — QK^T, softmax, PV — happen in a single CUDA kernel launch, eliminating the overhead of multiple kernel launches and HBM round trips between steps.
Subsequent versions further optimise work partitioning across warps (FA2) and exploit WGMMA/TMA instructions on H100 (FA3), achieving 70–85% of theoretical peak FLOP utilization on A100/H100.
The correctness of the tiling approach can be verified by comparing the output of the Python reference implementation against standard PyTorch attention on random inputs:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def standard_attention(Q, K, V):
    """Reference: full N×N materialisation."""
    d = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) / (d ** 0.5)
    weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    return weights @ V

def flash_attention_reference(Q, K, V, Br=64, Bc=64):
    """Tiled online-softmax attention (from earlier code, single head [N, d])."""
    N, d = Q.shape
    scale = d ** -0.5
    O = torch.zeros(N, d, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)
    l = torch.zeros(N, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)
    m = torch.full((N,), float("-inf"), dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)

    Tr = (N + Br - 1) // Br
    Tc = (N + Bc - 1) // Bc

    for i in range(Tr):
        Qi = Q[i*Br:(i+1)*Br]
        Oi = O[i*Br:(i+1)*Br].clone()
        li = l[i*Br:(i+1)*Br].clone()
        mi = m[i*Br:(i+1)*Br].clone()

        for j in range(Tc):
            Kj = K[j*Bc:(j+1)*Bc]
            Vj = V[j*Bc:(j+1)*Bc]
            Sij = (Qi @ Kj.T) * scale
            mij = Sij.max(dim=-1).values
            m_new = torch.maximum(mi, mij)
            Pij = torch.exp(Sij - m_new.unsqueeze(-1))
            lij = Pij.sum(dim=-1)
            alpha = torch.exp(mi - m_new)
            Oi = alpha.unsqueeze(-1) * Oi + Pij @ Vj
            li = alpha * li + lij
            mi = m_new

        O[i*Br:(i+1)*Br] = Oi / li.unsqueeze(-1)

    return O

torch.manual_seed(42)
N, d = 256, 64
Q = torch.randn(N, d)
K = torch.randn(N, d)
V = torch.randn(N, d)

out_std   = standard_attention(Q.unsqueeze(0), K.unsqueeze(0), V.unsqueeze(0)).squeeze(0)
out_flash = flash_attention_reference(Q, K, V, Br=32, Bc=32)

max_diff = (out_std - out_flash).abs().max().item()
print(f"Max absolute diff: {max_diff:.2e}")  # expect < 1e-5
assert max_diff < 1e-4, f"Implementation mismatch: {max_diff}"
print("Reference implementation matches standard attention ✓")
The backward pass of standard attention requires storing the N×N probability matrix P to compute gradients. FlashAttention avoids this with
: during the backward pass, the forward tiling is re-run to reconstruct the local P blocks on the fly, trading extra compute for O(N d) memory instead of O(N²).

This is gradient checkpointing applied at the attention level. The memory saving is the same as the forward pass: O(N²) → O(N d). The extra compute cost in the backward pass is one additional forward sweep through Q, K, V, which is small relative to the backward FLOPs.
FlashAttention stores only the log-sum-exp values L = m + log(l) per row (size N) to rescale gradients efficiently. This avoids re-running the full forward sweep without those values:

attrs.latex: L_i = m_i + \log(l_i) \in \mathbb{R}^N \quad \text{(stored from forward, size } O(N) \text{)}

content.text: The backward pass uses L to compute the softmax gradient without materialising P:
is a scalar per row. The backward CUDA kernel tiles Q, K, V and the output gradient dO identically to the forward, recomputing each P block locally.

Decoder-only language models use causal (lower-triangular) masking to prevent each token from attending to future tokens. Standard attention applies a mask to S before softmax. FlashAttention handles masking at the tile level:
Entire tile is masked to −∞; skip the
computation for this tile (zero contribution). This saves ~50% of the total computation for autoregressive decoding.
Mixed — apply the causal mask within the tile before computing the local softmax.

def flash_causal_attention(Q, K, V, Br=64, Bc=64):
    """
    Causal (lower-triangular) masked FlashAttention.
    Position i attends only to positions j <= i.
    """
    N, d = Q.shape
    scale = d ** -0.5
    O = torch.zeros(N, d, dtype=Q.dtype)
    l = torch.zeros(N, dtype=Q.dtype)
    m = torch.full((N,), float("-inf"), dtype=Q.dtype)

    Tr = (N + Br - 1) // Br
    Tc = (N + Bc - 1) // Bc

    for i in range(Tr):
        row_start = i * Br
        row_end   = min((i + 1) * Br, N)
        Qi = Q[row_start:row_end]
        Oi = O[row_start:row_end].clone()
        li = l[row_start:row_end].clone()
        mi = m[row_start:row_end].clone()
        rows = torch.arange(row_start, row_end).unsqueeze(1)  # [Br, 1]

        for j in range(Tc):
            col_start = j * Bc
            col_end   = min((j + 1) * Bc, N)

            # Skip tiles entirely above the diagonal
            if col_start > row_end - 1:
                break

            Kj = K[col_start:col_end]
            Vj = V[col_start:col_end]
            Sij = (Qi @ Kj.T) * scale         # [Br, Bc]

            # Apply causal mask inside diagonal tiles
            cols = torch.arange(col_start, col_end).unsqueeze(0)  # [1, Bc]
            mask = cols > rows                 # True where j > i (future positions)
            Sij = Sij.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))

            mij = Sij.max(dim=-1).values
            m_new = torch.maximum(mi, mij)
            Pij = torch.exp(Sij - m_new.unsqueeze(-1))
            lij = Pij.sum(dim=-1)
            alpha = torch.exp(mi - m_new)
            Oi = alpha.unsqueeze(-1) * Oi + Pij @ Vj
            li = alpha * li + lij
            mi = m_new

        O[row_start:row_end] = Oi / li.unsqueeze(-1)

    return O
package provides optimised CUDA kernels. For most use cases, the easiest entry point is via PyTorch's built-in scaled_dot_product_attention, which selects FlashAttention automatically when available:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# PyTorch 2.0+ — uses FlashAttention when available, falls back to efficient_attention or math
# Q, K, V: [batch, heads, seq, head_dim]
output = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=True)

# Explicit flash-attn package (more control, required for non-standard configs)
from flash_attn import flash_attn_func, flash_attn_varlen_func

# Standard: Q, K, V shape [batch, seqlen, nheads, headdim]
out = flash_attn_func(Q, K, V, causal=True)

# Variable-length sequences in a single batch (packed / unpadded)
# cu_seqlens_q/k: cumulative sequence lengths [batch+1], dtype int32
out, softmax_lse = flash_attn_varlen_func(
    q_flat, k_flat, v_flat,
    cu_seqlens_q=cu_seqlens,
    cu_seqlens_k=cu_seqlens,
    max_seqlen_q=max_seqlen,
    max_seqlen_k=max_seqlen,
    causal=True,
    return_attn_probs=False,
)
variant is important for training: packing variable-length sequences into a single batch without padding eliminates wasted computation on pad tokens and further improves GPU utilisation.

IO-aware attention recognises that standard attention's bottleneck is HBM bandwidth, not arithmetic throughput. By tiling the computation into blocks that fit in SRAM and using online softmax to merge the two softmax passes into one streaming pass, FlashAttention reduces HBM IO from

, shifting the kernel from memory-bound to compute-bound. The improvement is proportional to M/d — larger SRAM or smaller head dimension yields greater benefit. At sequence length 4096, practical speedups of 5–9× over standard attention have been reported, with no accuracy loss. This efficiency gain is the foundation for training and serving long-context models.
